highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.191 Released
highlights.summary: This release adds `/rewind`, a handy command that lets you resume a conversation from before you ran `/clear` — great if you accidentally wiped context you still needed. It also fixes an annoying scroll jump bug during streaming responses and resolves an issue where stopped background agents would unexpectedly restart.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.187 Released
highlights.summary: This update introduces a `sandbox.credentials` setting that prevents sandboxed commands from accessing your credential files and secret environment variables, improving security for automated workflows. Organizations can now restrict which models are available in Claude Code, enforced across the model picker, CLI flags, and environment variables.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.186 Released
highlights.summary: You can now authenticate MCP servers directly from the CLI using `claude mcp login <name>` and `claude mcp logout <name>`, without needing to open the interactive `/mcp` menu — especially useful when working over SSH with `--no-browser`. The `/workflows` agent view also gains status filtering (press `f`) to help you manage multiple agents more easily.

highlights.title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
highlights.summary: Recall is an open-source tool that gives Claude Code persistent, local memory about your project so it doesn't lose context between sessions. This is particularly useful for long-running projects where re-explaining architecture or conventions every session wastes time. Worth checking out if you find yourself repeatedly priming Claude with the same background information.

highlights.title: The text in Claude Code's "Extended Thinking" output is not authentic
highlights.summary: This article argues that the reasoning shown in Claude Code's Extended Thinking mode isn't a true window into the model's internal thought process, but rather a generated narrative that may not reflect actual computation. For developers relying on Extended Thinking to audit Claude's reasoning, this is an important caveat to keep in mind. Understanding this distinction helps set realistic expectations when using Extended Thinking for debugging or verification.

highlights.title: From Zero to Claude Code in 19 Minutes (no code)
highlights.summary: A quick beginner-friendly video that walks you through setting up and using Claude Code without any prior coding experience, in under 20 minutes. It's an ideal starting point if you've just heard about Claude Code and want to see it in action before committing to a longer course. Watch it alongside the VS Code extension to follow along hands-on.

highlights.title: Agent Rigor – Stop your AI coding assistant from doom-looping
highlights.summary: Agent Rigor is an open-source tool designed to detect and break the "doom loop" pattern where AI coding agents get stuck repeating failed attempts without making progress. If you've ever watched Claude Code spin in circles trying to fix a test, this tool adds guardrails to interrupt and recover gracefully. It's a practical safety net for anyone running long autonomous coding sessions.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes
highlights.summary: This YouTube tutorial offers a focused 30-minute deep dive into Claude Code's most powerful features, making it a great next step after the basics. It covers practical workflows and tips that help intermediate users get more out of daily coding sessions. Ideal if you've already done a quick intro video and want to level up your usage.

tips_of_the_day: Use `/rewind` after a `/clear` if you realise you still needed that conversation context — it brings back your history without having to start from scratch.
If you work over SSH or in a headless environment, authenticate MCP servers using `claude mcp login <name> --no-browser` to complete the auth flow via stdin instead of a browser popup.

Set a brief `CLAUDE.md` file at the root of each project with your key architectural decisions and coding conventions — tools like Recall and built-in project memory rely on this file to maintain context across sessions, saving you from re-explaining your stack every time.
